g.data-narration: IPv4 is the fundamental protocol enabling end-to-end communication across interconnected networks worldwide.
The IPv4 header contains 14 fixed fields plus optional fields, each serving a specific purpose in packet delivery.
The IPv4 header is 20 bytes minimum, extending to 60 bytes with optional fields. Each row represents 32 bits.
Version field is always 4 for IPv4. IHL indicates header length in 32-bit words, ranging from 5 to 15 words.
g.text: Fields: Version, IHL, ToS, Total Length

g.data-narration: Identification uniquely identifies each datagram. Flags control fragmentation: DF prevents it, MF marks more fragments.
TTL acts as a hop counter to prevent infinite loops. Protocol identifies the upper layer. Checksum validates the header.
Source and Destination IP addresses are 32-bit addresses. Options field is optional and extends header to 60 bytes max.
g.text: Fields: Source IP, Destination IP, Options

g.data-narration: Fragmentation breaks large datagrams into smaller pieces when crossing networks with smaller Maximum Transmission Units.
g.text: MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) - Maximum packet size per network:
When datagram MTU > outgoing link MTU → Fragment!

g.data-narration: During fragmentation, each fragment gets its own header but shares the Identification. Fragment offset shows position in original data.
Reassembly happens only at the destination host, not at routers. The ID, offset, and MF flag help reconstruct the original datagram.
Let's calculate fragments for a 4000-byte datagram crossing a 1500-byte MTU link. We need 3 fragments.
When TTL decrements from 64 to 63, the checksum must be recalculated. Routers update both TTL and checksum.
IPv4 addresses are 32-bit, divided into classes A-E. Classes A-C for unicast, D for multicast, E reserved.
Special addresses include 0.0.0.0 for this network, 127.x.x.x for loopback, and 255.255.255.255 for limited broadcast.
